news_summary: Today's environmental news highlights progress in sustainable energy development, groundbreaking scientific discoveries, marine ecosystem recovery, and innovative efforts to protect heritage sites from climate change.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les progrès réalisés en matière de développement des énergies durables, les découvertes scientifiques révolutionnaires, la restauration des écosystèmes marins et les initiatives innovantes visant à protéger les sites du patrimoine contre le changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan los avances en el desarrollo de la energía sostenible, los descubrimientos científicos revolucionarios, la recuperación de los ecosistemas marinos y las iniciativas innovadoras para proteger los lugares patrimoniales frente al cambio climático.

articles.title: Powering resilience: How Indonesia is building a more secure energy future
articles.summary: As countries grapple with energy price shocks, supply disruptions and the growing impacts of climate change, energy security has become a pressing concern.
articles.content:
In Indonesia, United Nations agencies have worked alongside the Government to build a more reliable and resilient energy system which is also helping communities to adapt to a changing climate.

Modernizing the electricity grid

A reliable electricity grid is the backbone of energy security. To prepare for growing demand and integrate more renewable energy, the United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS) is working with the state-owned electricity company PLN to identify more than two gigawatts of solar energy potential that could be connected to the grid.

Solar energy plus a new control centre will strengthen grid stability, reduce outages and lessen dependence on fossil fuels.

UN Indonesia

Using energy more efficiently

One of the simplest ways to improve energy security is to reduce network losses.

Working with government partners, UNOPS has supported energy-efficiency audits and policy reforms. These efforts help households, businesses and industries to optimize electricity use, which in turn lowers demand across the grid, ensuring more stable and affordable supply.

Making industry less energy intensive

Indonesia's industrial sector is one of the country's largest energy consumers, making efficiency gains particularly important.

The United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO) is helping some of Indonesia's most energy-intensive industries cut energy use while improving productivity.

In the fertilizer sector, resource-efficient and cleaner production measures supported by UNIDO have helped participating companies avoid an estimated 328,000 tonnes of carbon emissions annually while reducing operating costs.

UNIDO is also supporting the Ministry of Industry in developing eco-industrial parks, promoting energy efficiency, renewable energy use, wastewater recycling and improved waste management.

Unlocking finance for the energy transition

Indonesia has ambitious energy goals, including adding almost 43 gigawatts of renewable energy capacity by 2034.

Achieving this target will require up to $188 billion in investment.

© UNDP

To attract capital for renewable energy projects, the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) is working with the Government to develop innovative financing mechanisms, including blended finance approaches that combine public and private investment.

Supporting workers through the transition

Building a secure energy future is not only about technology and infrastructure. It also depends on people.

The International Labour Organization (ILO) is helping regions across Indonesia prepare for the social and economic impacts of the energy transition. This includes identifying new employment opportunities, supporting skills development and protecting workers whose livelihoods may be affected by changes in the energy sector.

By planning ahead, these initiatives help ensure that the transition strengthens communities rather than leaving workers behind.

Bringing cleaner energy to coastal communities

For many fishing communities, of which there are many in the Indonesia archipelago, rising fuel costs can threaten livelihoods.

UNDP has helped more than 2,400 small-scale fishers adopt cleaner technologies, including solar-powered boat engines, environmentally friendly cooler boxes and improved cold-storage systems.

© UNIC Jakarta

These innovations reduce fuel consumption, lower operating costs and make incomes less vulnerable to energy price fluctuations.

Fishers have also received training to operate and maintain the equipment, helping ensure long-term benefits.

Building resilient communities in remote areas

In some parts of Indonesia, communities remain far from the national electricity grid.

To address this challenge, UNDP supported the installation of 1.1 megawatts of solar mini-grid capacity across 22 remote villages, providing reliable electricity to more than 3,400 households and public facilities.

The systems have reduced energy costs by up to 40 per cent while lowering carbon emissions by an estimated 1,800 tonnes annually.

Local operators, nearly half of them women, have been trained to manage and maintain the systems, helping ensure reliable access to power over the long term.

At the same time, UNOPS has supported the Ministry of Environment's Climate Village initiative in South Sumatra, where around 285,000 residents have received training on renewable energy, low-emission technologies and circular economy practices.

Empowering women through clean energy

Women have a key role to play in Indonesia's energy transition.

UN Women and the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) have helped more than 400 women entrepreneurs gain access to green finance and adopt climate-smart technologies.

© KUMPUL

These include solar drying systems, biogas cooking solutions and solar-powered water pumps that can lower energy costs while supporting sustainable livelihoods.

By expanding economic opportunities and increasing access to clean energy technologies, these programmes help ensure that the benefits of the energy transition reach more people.

‘Groundbreaking work’

“From grid modernization to industrial efficiency, from securing funding to community-based solar systems, the UN is partnering with the Government to support its groundbreaking work in strengthening the country's energy security,” said Gita Sabharwal, United Nations Resident Coordinator in Indonesia.

“These initiatives reduce dependence on volatile global energy markets, boost supply reliability, and ensure that communities, from major industrial centres to remote villages, are contributing to a resilient future.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Indonesia's comprehensive efforts, supported by UN agencies, to build a secure, resilient, and clean energy future. It details significant initiatives including grid modernization, renewable energy integration, energy efficiency in industry, financing mechanisms, and community empowerment, all with broad societal and environmental benefits. The story highlights measurable impacts such as carbon emissions reductions, cost savings, and social inclusion, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial positive real-world impact and scope.
articles.personality_title: Indonesia and UN join forces to build a stronger, cleaner energy future
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Indonesia faces challenges like rising energy prices and climate change, which make it important to have a reliable and clean energy system.

**What happened** – United Nations agencies are working with the Indonesian government to improve the country’s energy system. They are modernizing the electricity grid to add more solar power, helping industries use energy more efficiently, and supporting cleaner energy in fishing communities and remote villages. They are also creating new ways to finance renewable energy projects and helping workers adjust to changes in the energy sector.

**Impact** – These efforts are reducing power outages, cutting carbon emissions, lowering energy costs, and creating new jobs. For example, solar mini-grids now provide electricity to over 3,400 households in remote villages, and cleaner technology in fertilizer factories cuts 328,000 tonnes of carbon emissions each year. Women entrepreneurs are gaining access to green tools that help their businesses and communities.

**What’s next step** – Indonesia plans to add nearly 43 gigawatts of renewable energy by 2034. The UN and government will keep working to attract investments, train local workers, and expand clean energy projects so more people can benefit from a stable and eco-friendly power supply.

**One-sentence takeaway** – With UN support, Indonesia is building a reliable and cleaner energy system that helps communities, cuts emissions, and prepares the country for a sustainable future.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Indonésie et l’ONU unissent leurs forces pour un avenir énergétique plus solide et propre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Indonésie fait face à des défis comme la hausse des prix de l’énergie et le changement climatique, ce qui rend important d’avoir un système énergétique fiable et propre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des agences des Nations Unies travaillent avec le gouvernement indonésien pour améliorer le système énergétique du pays. Elles modernisent le réseau électrique pour ajouter plus d’énergie solaire, aident les industries à utiliser l’énergie plus efficacement, et soutiennent une énergie plus propre dans les communautés de pêcheurs et les villages éloignés. Elles créent aussi de nouvelles façons de financer les projets d’énergie renouvelable et aident les travailleurs à s’adapter aux changements du secteur énergétique.

**Impact** – Ces efforts réduisent les coupures d’électricité, diminuent les émissions de carbone, baissent les coûts de l’énergie et créent de nouveaux emplois. Par exemple, des mini-réseaux solaires fournissent maintenant de l’électricité à plus de 3 400 foyers dans des villages éloignés, et des technologies plus propres dans les usines d’engrais réduisent de 328 000 tonnes les émissions annuelles de carbone. Des femmes entrepreneures ont accès à des outils verts qui aident leurs entreprises et leurs communautés.

**Prochaine étape** – L’Indonésie prévoit d’ajouter près de 43 gigawatts d’énergie renouvelable d’ici 2034. L’ONU et le gouvernement continueront à attirer des investissements, former les travailleurs locaux et étendre les projets d’énergie propre pour que plus de personnes bénéficient d’une fourniture d’électricité stable et respectueuse de l’environnement.

**Une phrase clé** – Avec le soutien de l’ONU, l’Indonésie construit un système énergétique fiable et plus propre qui aide les communautés, réduit les émissions et prépare le pays à un avenir durable.
articles.personality_title_es: Indonesia y la ONU trabajan juntos para un futuro energético más fuerte y limpio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Indonesia enfrenta desafíos como el aumento de los precios de la energía y el cambio climático, lo que hace importante tener un sistema energético confiable y limpio.

**Qué sucedió** – Agencias de las Naciones Unidas están colaborando con el gobierno de Indonesia para mejorar el sistema energético del país. Están modernizando la red eléctrica para agregar más energía solar, ayudando a las industrias a usar la energía de manera más eficiente y apoyando energía más limpia en comunidades pesqueras y aldeas remotas. También están creando nuevas formas de financiar proyectos de energía renovable y ayudando a los trabajadores a adaptarse a los cambios en el sector energético.

**Impacto** – Estos esfuerzos reducen los cortes de energía, bajan las emisiones de carbono, disminuyen los costos de energía y crean nuevos empleos. Por ejemplo, mini redes solares ahora proveen electricidad a más de 3,400 hogares en aldeas remotas, y la tecnología limpia en fábricas de fertilizantes reduce 328,000 toneladas de emisiones de carbono al año. Mujeres emprendedoras acceden a herramientas verdes que ayudan a sus negocios y comunidades.

**Próximo paso** – Indonesia planea añadir casi 43 gigavatios de energía renovable para 2034. La ONU y el gobierno seguirán trabajando para atraer inversiones, capacitar a los trabajadores locales y ampliar los proyectos de energía limpia para que más personas puedan beneficiarse de un suministro eléctrico estable y ecológico.

**Una frase para llevar** – Con el apoyo de la ONU, Indonesia está construyendo un sistema energético confiable y más limpio que ayuda a las comunidades, reduce emisiones y prepara al país para un futuro sostenible.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Indonesian archipelago at sunset, featuring interconnected solar panels floating above calm coastal waters near fishing boats powered by glowing solar engines, with stylized silhouettes of diverse hands—some holding tools, others nurturing seedlings—rising from lush green industrial parks and remote village rooftops, symbolizing community-driven renewable energy, resilience, and inclusive progress.

articles.title: Scientists open a million-year-old time capsule hidden beneath New Zealand
articles.summary: A cave in New Zealand has yielded fossils from a lost ecosystem that existed about 1 million years ago, including a possible flying ancestor of the kākāpō. The discovery reveals that volcanoes and climate upheaval were reshaping the country’s wildlife and driving extinctions long before humans arrived.
articles.content:
A remarkable fossil discovery inside a cave near Waitomo on New Zealand's North Island is giving scientists an unprecedented look at a long vanished ecosystem. Researchers from Australia and New Zealand have uncovered the remains of ancient birds and frogs that lived around 1 million years ago, including a previously unknown relative of the iconic kākāpō.

The find marks the first time scientists have recovered a large collection of terrestrial vertebrate fossils from this period in New Zealand's history. Preserved within the cave were fossils belonging to 12 bird species and four frog species, offering a rare snapshot of a world that existed hundreds of thousands of years before humans reached the islands.

The research, published in Alcheringa: An Australasian Journal of Palaeontology, suggests that New Zealand's wildlife was already undergoing dramatic changes long before human settlement. Powerful volcanic eruptions and rapid climate shifts repeatedly reshaped habitats, driving extinctions and opening opportunities for new species to evolve.

Ancient Birds Lost to Time

Lead author Associate Professor Trevor Worthy of Flinders University says the fossils reveal a bird community unlike anything seen in New Zealand today.

"This is a newly recognized avifauna for New Zealand, one that was replaced by the one humans encountered a million years later," says Associate Professor Worthy, from the College of Science and Engineering at Flinders University.

"This remarkable find suggests our ancient forests were once home to a diverse group of birds that did not survive the next million years."

In biology, the term "avifauna" refers to the collection of bird species living in a particular place and time. The fossils indicate that the birds inhabiting New Zealand a million years ago were substantially different from those present when people eventually arrived.

The study involved paleontologists from Flinders University and Canterbury Museum, as well as volcanologists Joel Baker of the University of Auckland and Simon Barker of Victoria University of Wellington.

According to the researchers, approximately 33-50% of species disappeared during the million years before humans reached Aotearoa New Zealand.

Volcanoes and Climate Change Reshaped Ecosystems

Scientists believe these losses were largely caused by natural environmental upheaval.

"These extinctions were driven by relatively rapid climate shifts and cataclysmic volcanic eruptions," says co-author Dr. Paul Scofield, Senior Curator of Natural History at Canterbury Museum.

The discovery helps fill one of the largest gaps in New Zealand's fossil record.

"From our excavations at St Bathans in Central Otago over many years, we have a snapshot of life in Aotearoa between 20 and 16 million years ago. These new findings cast light on the 15 million year period from then to 1 million years ago, which is largely absent from New Zealand's fossil record," says Dr. Scofield.

"This wasn't a missing chapter in New Zealand's ancient history, it was a missing volume."

Fossils are often compared to pages in Earth's history book. In this case, researchers say they have uncovered an entire section of that story that was previously unknown.

A Possible Flying Ancestor of the Kākāpō

One of the most exciting discoveries is a newly identified parrot species called Strigops insulaborealis. It is an ancient relative of the kākāpō, one of New Zealand's most famous birds.

Today, the kākāpō is the world's only flightless parrot. It is also one of the heaviest parrots and is known for its unusual nighttime lifestyle. However, the newly discovered ancestor may have been very different.

Analysis of the fossilized bones suggests it had weaker legs than modern kākāpō. Because today's birds rely heavily on their strong legs and climbing ability, researchers think the ancient species may have spent less time climbing and possibly retained the ability to fly.

Additional research will be needed to determine whether it truly could take to the air.

The cave also contained fossils from an extinct ancestor of the takahē, another distinctive New Zealand bird. Researchers also identified an extinct pigeon species closely related to Australia's bronzewing pigeons.

"The shifting forest and shrubland habitats forced a reset of the bird populations," adds Dr. Scofield.

"We believe this was a major driver for the evolutionary diversification of birds and other fauna in the North Island."

Volcanic Ash Helps Date the Fossils

One reason the discovery is so important is that scientists can determine its age with unusual precision.

The fossils were trapped between two layers of volcanic ash preserved inside the cave. One ash layer came from an eruption about 1.55 million years ago. The second was produced by a massive eruption approximately 1 million years ago.

This natural geological sandwich provides clear age limits for the fossils.

Researchers say the younger eruption likely covered much of the North Island in meters of ash. While rain and erosion eventually removed much of that material, some remained protected inside caves.

The older ash layer also reveals something else remarkable. It shows that the fossil site is the oldest known cave on New Zealand's North Island.

Rewriting New Zealand's Natural History

Associate Professor Worthy says the fossils provide a crucial benchmark for understanding how New Zealand's wildlife evolved.

The fossils "provide a critical, missing baseline for New Zealand's natural history."

For many years, scientists focused primarily on the ecological changes that occurred after humans arrived in New Zealand roughly 750 years ago. The new evidence shows that powerful natural forces had already been transforming the islands' wildlife for hundreds of thousands of years.

"For decades, the extinction of New Zealand's birds was viewed primarily through the lens of human arrival 750 years ago. This study proves that natural forces like super-volcanoes and dramatic climate shifts were already sculpting the unique identity of our wildlife over a million years ago."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of million-year-old fossils in New Zealand, revealing how natural forces shaped wildlife long before humans arrived. This finding fills a major gap in the fossil record, provides new insights into evolutionary history, and enhances understanding of environmental and ecological changes with broad scientific and public interest.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover million-year-old fossils revealing lost New Zealand wildlife
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied New Zealand’s unique animals, but there was a big gap in knowledge about what lived there between 20 million and 1 million years ago. Fossils from this time were rare, leaving questions about how the country’s wildlife changed before humans arrived.

**What happened** – Researchers found fossils inside a cave near Waitomo, North Island, dating from about 1 million years ago. These fossils include 12 bird species and four frog species, among them a new ancient relative of the kākāpō, a famous flightless parrot. The fossils were preserved between two layers of volcanic ash, allowing scientists to date them precisely.

**Impact** – This discovery is the first large collection of land animal fossils from this period in New Zealand. It shows that volcanoes and climate changes caused many species to disappear long before humans came. The ancient birds found were very different from those known today, revealing a lost ecosystem and helping fill a major missing chapter in New Zealand’s natural history.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying the fossils to understand more about the ancient birds’ habits, including whether the kākāpō ancestor could fly. They also hope to learn how environmental changes shaped the evolution of New Zealand’s unique animals over millions of years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Fossils from a million years ago in New Zealand reveal a vanished world shaped by volcanoes and climate, offering new insights into the country’s ancient wildlife before humans arrived.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des fossiles vieux d’un million d’années révélant la faune disparue de Nouvelle-Zélande
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps les animaux uniques de Nouvelle-Zélande, mais il manquait des informations sur la faune entre 20 millions et 1 million d’années. Les fossiles de cette période étaient rares, laissant des questions sur l’évolution avant l’arrivée des humains.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé des fossiles dans une grotte près de Waitomo, sur l’île du Nord, datant d’environ 1 million d’années. Ces fossiles comprennent 12 espèces d’oiseaux et 4 espèces de grenouilles, dont un ancien parent inconnu du kākāpō, un perroquet célèbre incapable de voler. Les fossiles ont été conservés entre deux couches de cendres volcaniques, permettant une datation précise.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est la première grande collection de fossiles d’animaux terrestres de cette période en Nouvelle-Zélande. Elle montre que les volcans et les changements climatiques ont causé la disparition de nombreuses espèces bien avant l’arrivée des humains. Les anciens oiseaux découverts étaient très différents de ceux d’aujourd’hui, révélant un écosystème perdu et comblant un chapitre important de l’histoire naturelle du pays.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier les fossiles pour mieux comprendre le mode de vie des anciens oiseaux, notamment si l’ancêtre du kākāpō pouvait voler. Ils espèrent aussi découvrir comment les changements environnementaux ont influencé l’évolution des animaux uniques de Nouvelle-Zélande sur des millions d’années.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des fossiles vieux d’un million d’années en Nouvelle-Zélande révèlent un monde disparu façonné par les volcans et le climat, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur la faune ancienne avant l’arrivée des humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren fósiles de un millón de años que revelan la fauna perdida de Nueva Zelanda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo los animales únicos de Nueva Zelanda, pero faltaba información sobre qué vivía allí entre hace 20 millones y 1 millón de años. Los fósiles de ese período eran raros, dejando preguntas sobre cómo cambió la fauna antes de que llegaran los humanos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron fósiles dentro de una cueva cerca de Waitomo, en la Isla Norte, que datan de hace aproximadamente 1 millón de años. Estos fósiles incluyen 12 especies de aves y 4 de ranas, entre ellas un nuevo pariente antiguo del kākāpō, un famoso loro que no puede volar. Los fósiles estaban atrapados entre dos capas de ceniza volcánica, lo que permitió datarlos con precisión.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es la primera gran colección de fósiles de animales terrestres de ese período en Nueva Zelanda. Muestra que los volcanes y los cambios climáticos causaron muchas extinciones mucho antes de la llegada humana. Las aves antiguas eran muy diferentes a las actuales, revelando un ecosistema perdido y llenando un gran vacío en la historia natural del país.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando los fósiles para entender más sobre los hábitos de las aves antiguas, incluyendo si el ancestro del kākāpō podía volar. También esperan aprender cómo los cambios ambientales moldearon la evolución de los animales únicos de Nueva Zelanda durante millones de años.

**Resumen en una frase** – Fósiles de un millón de años en Nueva Zelanda revelan un mundo perdido moldeado por volcanes y clima, ofreciendo nuevas ideas sobre la antigua fauna antes de la llegada humana.
articles.image_prompt: An ancient cave interior softly illuminated by warm, natural light filtering through cracks, revealing detailed fossilized bones of unique birds—including a flight-capable parrot ancestor of the kākāpō—and frogs embedded in layers of volcanic ash, with subtle silhouettes of erupting volcanoes and shifting forest landscapes visible through the cave entrance, all rendered in gentle earth tones and muted greens.

articles.title: Seabed damaged by fishing showing signs of recovery
articles.summary: Campaigners say improvements to the area trashed by illegal dredging show that marine protection works.
articles.content: "Cod, of course, is one of the species we all want to see recovered in these areas. Cod once were here in such abundance that they were caught commercially and landed into the harbours all around the coastline," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the recovery of seabed areas damaged by illegal fishing, demonstrating that marine protection efforts are effective. This has broad environmental significance and positive impact on marine biodiversity and fisheries.
articles.personality_title: Seabed damaged by fishing shows signs of recovery
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some areas of the seabed were badly damaged by illegal fishing, especially by dredging, which scrapes the ocean floor and harms marine life. These areas had fewer fish and less healthy habitats.

**What happened** – Recently, experts and campaigners noticed that the seabed in these damaged areas is starting to recover. Fish like cod, which used to be very common and important for local fishing, are coming back. This improvement happened after rules were put in place to protect these parts of the ocean from harmful fishing methods.

**Impact** – This recovery shows that protecting the ocean can help damaged underwater environments heal. It means fish populations can grow again, which is good for the environment and for people who rely on fishing. It also proves that stopping illegal fishing can make a real difference.

**What's next step** – Continued monitoring will help scientists understand how fast the seabed and fish populations recover. Authorities may strengthen protections and encourage more sustainable fishing to keep the seabed healthy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Protected seabed areas damaged by illegal fishing are showing real signs of recovery, proving marine protection efforts can restore ocean life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le fond marin abîmé par la pêche montre des signes de récupération
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certaines zones du fond marin ont été gravement endommagées par la pêche illégale, notamment par le dragage, qui gratte le sol océanique et nuit à la vie marine. Ces zones comptaient moins de poissons et avaient des habitats moins sains.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, des experts et des militants ont remarqué que le fond marin dans ces zones endommagées commence à se restaurer. Des poissons comme la morue, autrefois très présents et importants pour la pêche locale, reviennent. Cette amélioration est arrivée après la mise en place de règles pour protéger ces parties de l’océan contre les méthodes de pêche nuisibles.

**Impact** – Cette récupération montre que protéger l’océan peut aider les environnements sous-marins endommagés à guérir. Cela signifie que les populations de poissons peuvent à nouveau croître, ce qui est bénéfique pour l’environnement et pour les personnes qui dépendent de la pêche. Cela prouve aussi que stopper la pêche illégale peut faire une vraie différence.

**Prochaine étape** – Le suivi continu aidera les scientifiques à comprendre la vitesse de récupération du fond marin et des populations de poissons. Les autorités pourraient renforcer les protections et encourager une pêche plus durable pour garder le fond marin en bonne santé.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les zones protégées du fond marin endommagées par la pêche illégale montrent de vrais signes de récupération, prouvant que les efforts de protection marine peuvent restaurer la vie océanique.
articles.personality_title_es: El fondo marino dañado por la pesca muestra señales de recuperación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunas áreas del fondo marino fueron gravemente dañadas por la pesca ilegal, especialmente por el dragado, que raspa el suelo oceánico y afecta la vida marina. Estas zonas tenían menos peces y hábitats menos saludables.

**Qué sucedió** – Recientemente, expertos y activistas notaron que el fondo marino en estas áreas dañadas está empezando a recuperarse. Peces como el bacalao, que antes eran muy comunes e importantes para la pesca local, están regresando. Esta mejora ocurrió después de que se implementaron reglas para proteger estas partes del océano de métodos de pesca dañinos.

**Impacto** – Esta recuperación muestra que proteger el océano puede ayudar a que los ambientes submarinos dañados sanen. Esto significa que las poblaciones de peces pueden crecer de nuevo, lo cual es bueno para el medio ambiente y para las personas que dependen de la pesca. También demuestra que detener la pesca ilegal puede hacer una diferencia real.

**Próximo paso** – El monitoreo continuo ayudará a los científicos a entender qué tan rápido se recuperan el fondo marino y las poblaciones de peces. Las autoridades podrían fortalecer las protecciones y fomentar una pesca más sostenible para mantener el fondo marino saludable.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Las áreas protegidas del fondo marino dañadas por la pesca ilegal muestran señales reales de recuperación, demostrando que los esfuerzos de protección marina pueden restaurar la vida oceánica.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently recovering seabed with vibrant patches of seaweed and coral, surrounded by a small school of graceful cod fish swimming freely above the textured ocean floor, rendered in soft natural blues, greens, and earthy tones.

articles.title: Why heritage sites are at risk in a warming world — and how to save them
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01956-0As rising seas and intensifying disasters threaten historic sites worldwide, new ways to understand, preserve and adapt these places are needed urgently.
articles.content:
The Future of the Past: When Cultural Heritage Meets Climate Change Thijs Weststeijn Polity (2026)

In 2019, unprecedented flooding in Venice submerged more than 80% of the historic city, prompting Italy to declare a state of emergency. That same year — and in 2025 — wildfires in California burned thousands of hectares of land. The flames came dangerously close to the Getty Villa, a museum in Los Angeles that houses one of the world’s most important collections of ancient Greek and Roman art.

These incidents illustrate a troubling reality: cultural heritage sites, once seen as enduring fixtures of human achievement, are increasingly vulnerable to climate-driven disasters. The idea that humanity’s greatest accomplishments are now threatened by our own failures is a theme that runs through art historian Thijs Weststeijn’s latest book. The Future of the Past offers a timely and accessible introduction to the growing tension between heritage preservation and a rapidly changing climate.

Sustainability or dystopia? What past patterns tell us about where society is heading

Weststeijn lays out the scale of the challenge by examining cultural and historical sites around the world that are increasingly exposed to climate pressures, ranging from thawing permafrost to rising sea levels. One striking example is the Moai, the monumental stone figures of Rapa Nui (also known as Easter Island), many of which stand along the coast and are threatened by erosion and encroaching waters. More broadly, Weststeijn highlights a recurring paradox. Societies that once depended on the sea for mobility, trade and cultural exchange — such as those on the Swahili coast in East Africa — now find their heritage sites undermined by rising oceans and coastal instability. The very environments that enabled these cultures to flourish are becoming sources of risk.

Origins of UNESCO

Alongside these case studies, Weststeijn situates the discussion in a broader intellectual history of heritage itself. He traces the origins of key concepts such as ‘world heritage’ — a term used by the United Nations cultural organization UNESCO to designate places on Earth that are of outstanding universal value to humanity — and shows how they emerged mainly from Western, Eurocentric frameworks. This is evident in his account of UNESCO’s founding in 1945 after the destruction of cultural monuments and artwork during the Second World War prompted an international effort to identify, value and protect such sites. He argues that although this framework has been influential, it can also limit how we understand and respond to cultural loss in a rapidly changing climate.

Another concept Weststeijn highlights is ‘solastalgia’, a word related to nostalgia, which describes the emotional strain of witnessing one’s environment change or deteriorate. Used by the The Lancet in 2015, it offers a way to grasp the psychological toll of climate change, broadening heritage beyond physical sites to include lived experience and a sense of place (N. Watts et al. Lancet 386, 1861–1914; 2015).

The Getty Villa, a museum in Los Angeles, California, has been threatened by wildfires.Credit: Christina House/Los Angeles Times/Getty

The discussion of alternative conceptions of cultural significance lays the groundwork for a more nuanced exploration of emotive topics, such as adaptive reuse of historic sites, in which places might be altered or repurposed to maintain their relevance and to ensure their survival. At the core of these discussions is the difficult question of which heritage sites should be prioritized for protection as others face imminent loss. “The very concept of heritage is therefore in need of an overhaul, now that the ability to hand it on to subsequent generations can no longer be taken for granted,” Weststeijn writes.

Inspiring climate action

A recurring theme in Weststeijn’s book is that culture and heritage offer a powerful lens through which to understand — and respond to — the climate crisis. As he suggests, there is a profound need for “physical locations to which we can attach the stories we want to tell ourselves”, underscoring the communicative power of heritage sites in shaping public awareness and inspiring climate action.

As emotive places, cultural heritage sites provide a sense of continuity and stability in an increasingly volatile world, which makes their loss all the more acute. Although readers outside Europe might find the book’s focus on primarily European sites to be narrow, Weststeijn’s argument that these places function as proverbial canaries in the coal mine is persuasive. Their vulnerability acts as an early warning sign of the wider risks faced by heritage locations globally.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the urgent threat climate change poses to global cultural heritage sites and discusses innovative approaches to preserve and adapt these sites. It emphasizes the broad societal importance of heritage preservation as a means to inspire climate action and raise public awareness. The story presents a meaningful, tangible challenge with wide-reaching implications and offers a hopeful perspective on using heritage to motivate environmental responsibility.
articles.personality_title: Protecting heritage sites from climate threats to inspire action
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many important cultural sites around the world are being damaged by climate change. Flooding, wildfires, and rising seas are putting historic places at risk, showing that even long-lasting landmarks are vulnerable.

**What happened** – In recent years, Venice experienced severe flooding that covered most of the city, and wildfires in California came close to the Getty Villa museum, which holds ancient art. These events highlight the growing danger climate change poses to heritage sites. A new book by historian Thijs Weststeijn explains how climate change is putting many heritage sites, like the stone Moai statues on Easter Island, at risk and discusses the challenge of protecting them.

**Impact** – The book shows that climate change is changing the environments that helped many cultures thrive, making it harder to keep their history safe. It also explains how the idea of “world heritage” began after World War II to protect important places, but that this idea needs to change to fit today’s climate challenges. The emotional effect of losing these places is also important, as people feel sadness watching their environment change.

**What's next step** – Experts and communities will need to find new ways to protect and adapt heritage sites, possibly by changing how they are used or cared for. Deciding which sites to save first will be a difficult choice. The book encourages using heritage sites to tell stories that help people understand climate change and motivate action.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Climate change threatens historic places worldwide, but protecting and adapting these sites can help inspire people to act for the planet’s future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Protéger les sites patrimoniaux des menaces climatiques pour inspirer l’action
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux sites culturels importants dans le monde sont endommagés par le changement climatique. Inondations, incendies et montée des eaux mettent en danger des lieux historiques, montrant que même les monuments anciens sont vulnérables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ces dernières années, Venise a subi de graves inondations qui ont submergé la majeure partie de la ville, et des incendies en Californie ont menacé le musée Getty Villa, qui abrite des œuvres d’art anciennes. Ces événements montrent le danger croissant du changement climatique pour les sites patrimoniaux. Un nouveau livre de l’historien Thijs Weststeijn explique comment le climat met en péril de nombreux sites, comme les statues Moaï de l’île de Pâques, et parle du défi de leur protection.

**Impact** – Le livre montre que le changement climatique modifie les environnements qui ont permis à beaucoup de cultures de prospérer, rendant plus difficile la préservation de leur histoire. Il explique aussi comment le concept de « patrimoine mondial » est né après la Seconde Guerre mondiale pour protéger ces lieux, mais qu’il doit évoluer face aux défis climatiques actuels. La perte de ces sites affecte aussi les émotions, car les gens ressentent de la tristesse en voyant leur environnement changer.

**Prochaine étape** – Il faudra trouver de nouvelles façons de protéger et d’adapter les sites patrimoniaux, parfois en changeant leur usage ou leur entretien. Choisir quels sites sauver en priorité sera un choix difficile. Le livre encourage à utiliser ces sites pour raconter des histoires qui aident à comprendre le changement climatique et à agir.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Le changement climatique menace les lieux historiques dans le monde, mais leur protection et adaptation peuvent encourager les gens à agir pour l’avenir de la planète.
articles.personality_title_es: Proteger sitios patrimoniales de amenazas climáticas para inspirar acción
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos sitios culturales importantes en el mundo están siendo dañados por el cambio climático. Inundaciones, incendios y subida del nivel del mar ponen en riesgo lugares históricos, mostrando que incluso monumentos antiguos son vulnerables.

**Qué pasó** – En años recientes, Venecia sufrió inundaciones severas que cubrieron gran parte de la ciudad, y los incendios en California amenazaron el museo Getty Villa, que guarda arte antiguo. Estos eventos muestran el peligro creciente del cambio climático para los sitios patrimoniales. Un libro nuevo del historiador Thijs Weststeijn explica cómo el clima pone en riesgo muchos sitios, como las estatuas Moai en la Isla de Pascua, y habla del reto de protegerlos.

**Impacto** – El libro muestra que el cambio climático está cambiando los ambientes que permitieron que muchas culturas prosperaran, haciendo más difícil conservar su historia. También explica cómo la idea de “patrimonio mundial” surgió después de la Segunda Guerra Mundial para proteger estos lugares, pero que ahora debe cambiar para enfrentar los desafíos climáticos. La pérdida de estos sitios afecta las emociones, porque las personas sienten tristeza al ver su entorno cambiar.

**Próximo paso** – Expertos y comunidades tendrán que encontrar nuevas formas de proteger y adaptar los sitios patrimoniales, a veces cambiando su uso o cuidado. Decidir qué sitios salvar primero será una elección difícil. El libro anima a usar estos sitios para contar historias que ayuden a entender el cambio climático y motivar la acción.

**Mensaje en una frase** – El cambio climático amenaza lugares históricos en todo el mundo, pero proteger y adaptar estos sitios puede ayudar a inspirar a las personas a actuar por el futuro del planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of ancient stone Moai statues standing resiliently on a gently eroding coastline with rising seas, alongside a serene depiction of the historic Venice skyline partially submerged in calm floodwaters, connected by a glowing, protective aura symbolizing hope and preservation amid subtle hints of wildfire smoke in the distant sky.
